name: Atlantis Location Theories
description: Atlantis location theories represent diverse attempts to pinpoint the geographical site of the legendary island civilization described by Plato in his dialogues *Timaeus* and *Critias*. Plato described Atlantis as a powerful naval empire that existed "beyond the Pillars of Hercules" (generally interpreted as the Strait of Gibraltar) and was eventually swallowed by the sea after a failed attempt to conquer Athens. While mainstream scholarship largely views Atlantis as a philosophical allegory created by Plato to illustrate his political ideas, proponents of Atlantis location theories believe it was a real place. These theories vary wildly, proposing locations ranging from the Atlantic Ocean (the most common suggestion) to the Mediterranean Sea, Antarctica, the Baltic Sea, and even South America. Evidence cited by proponents includes alleged geological anomalies, similarities between ancient cultures on different continents, interpretations of ancient texts and maps, and speculative connections to mythological narratives. Mainstream historians and archaeologists generally dismiss these theories due to a lack of verifiable physical evidence, reliance on selective interpretation of data, and the absence of Atlantis in contemporary historical records outside of Plato's dialogues. Furthermore, the geological and oceanographic evidence often presented by proponents is typically explained by natural processes, and the cultural similarities can be attributed to independent development or later cultural exchange. The enduring appeal of Atlantis location theories lies in the human desire to uncover lost civilizations and hidden histories.
subtitle: Searching for Plato's lost island empire.

claims.claim: Atlantis was located in the Atlantic Ocean.
claims.evidence: Plato's description of its location "beyond the Pillars of Hercules"; Mid-Atlantic Ridge geological formations; alleged ancient maps depicting a large island.
claims.criticism: Lack of submerged city structures; geological evidence of gradual sea-level rise, not catastrophic sinking; misinterpretations of ancient maps.

claims.claim: Atlantis was located in the Mediterranean Sea (e.g., Santorini/Thera).
claims.evidence: The Minoan civilization's advanced culture and sudden collapse (possibly due to the Thera eruption); similarities between Minoan art and Plato's description of Atlantis; Plato's possible inspiration from Minoan history.
claims.criticism: Discrepancies in size and timeframe compared to Plato's account; lack of evidence for Minoan expansionism as described by Plato; Thera eruption's impact is well-documented, but not a complete disappearance.

claims.evidence: Piri Reis map depicting a coastline resembling Antarctica (before its official discovery); Charles Hapgood's theory of Earth crust displacement; alleged evidence of ancient civilization beneath the ice.
claims.criticism: Piri Reis map's interpretation is debated; Earth crust displacement theory is largely discredited; lack of conclusive archaeological evidence from Antarctica; the continent was likely ice-covered during the proposed Atlantean period.

keyFigures.name: Ignatius L. Donnelly
keyFigures.role: Author of *Atlantis: The Antediluvian World* (1882), a key proponent of the Atlantis theory.

keyFigures.name: Charles Hapgood
keyFigures.role: Advocate of the Earth crust displacement theory, linking Atlantis to Antarctica.

keyFigures.name: Kenneth Feder
keyFigures.role: Archaeologist and skeptic, author of *Frauds, Myths, and Mysteries: Science and Pseudoscience in Archaeology*

timeline.event: Plato writes *Timaeus* and *Critias*, introducing the Atlantis narrative.
Ignatius L. Donnelly publishes *Atlantis: The Antediluvian World*, popularizing Atlantis theories.
Various Atlantis location theories emerge, linking Atlantis to locations worldwide.

sources.author: Kenneth Feder
sources.work: Frauds, Myths, and Mysteries: Science and Pseudoscience in Archaeology
sources.title: Kenneth Feder
